MASTER CALENDAR MOTION ON AUG-07-2024 IN DEPT. 206, DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT'S MOTION TO CONTINUE TRIAL DATE WAS HEARD. LAUREN CERRI, ESQ. APPEARING FOR PLAINTIFF. HEATHER HENSLEY, ESQ. APPEARING FOR DEFENDANT. ARGUED AND THE COURT CONTINUED THE MATTER TO AUG-28-2024, AFTER THE MOTION FOR DISCOVERY SET FOR 08/21/24 IN DEPT. 302 IS HEARD AND DECIDED. IF THAT MOTION IS GRANTED, THEN THE COURT'S GUIDANCE TO COUNSEL IS TO FILE A STIPULATION WITH A NEW TRIAL DATE WITH A SEPARATE PROPOSED ORDER AND EMAIL DEPT. 206 AT DEPARTMENT206@SFTC.ORG. IF THE STIPULATION IS ON FILE, THEN NO APPEARANCES ARE REQUIRED ON 08/28/24. DISCOVERY WILL TRACK THE NEW TRIAL DATE. IF COUNSEL CANNOT AGREE ON A NEW TRIAL DATE, THEN APPEARANCES ARE REQUIRED ON AUG-28-2024 AT 9:30AM IN DEPT. 206. JUDGE: ANNE-CHRISTINE MASSULLO; MATTER NOT REPORTED (206)

MOTION TO CONTINUE JURY TRIAL
Off Calendar Aug-28-2024 Text Ruling

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Discovery
Off Calendar Aug-21-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT'S Motion For Discovery.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling. The motion is GRANTED. Under Code of Civil Procedure section 2017.220, subdivision a, "[i]n any civil action alleging conduct that constitutes . . . sexual assault, or sexual battery, any party seeking discovery concerning the plaintiff's sexual conduct with individuals other than the alleged perpetrator shall establish specific facts showing that there is good cause for that discovery, and that the matter sought to be discovered is relevant to the subject matter of the action and reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence." Defendant has satisfied that standard in light of Plaintiff's damages claims. (See Godwin Decl., Ex. D (declaration of mental health professional attesting to need for requested discovery); Vinson v. Superior Court (1987) 43 Cal.3d 833, 840-41 ["Plaintiff in the case at bar asserts that she continues to suffer diminished self-esteem, reduced motivation, sleeplessness, loss of appetite, fear, lessened ability to help others, loss of social contacts, anxiety, mental anguish, loss of reputation, and severe emotional distress. In their motion defendants pointed to these allegations. Because the truth of these claims is relevant to plaintiff's cause of action and justifying facts have been shown with specificity, good cause as to these assertions has been demonstrated."); compare Barrenda L. v. Superior Court (1998) 65 Cal.App.4th 794, 802 [reversing order granting discovery where "there is no declaration by a mental health professional that evidence of other sexual conduct would be relevant and necessary to a determination of the cause of petitioners' emotional distress"].) While Plaintiff argues Defendant should be required to show "extraordinary circumstances" to obtain the requested discovery, including "where (as here) the inquiry relates to remote or juvenile section conduct," the cases Plaintiff cites do not support that proposition, and the Supreme Court held in Doe v. Superior Court (2023) 15 Cal. 5th 40, 69 n.14, that "extraordinary circumstances" is not the appropriate test. Plaintiff also contends that Defendant's request is insufficiently narrowly tailored, but Defendant does not seek discovery into Plaintiff's entire sexual history, only the history of sexual abuse. Finally, this ruling relates only to discoverability, not admissibility, which separately must be addressed before any evidence relating to Plaintiff's history of sexual abuse may be used at trial.

Order signed in open court.

Judge Pro Tem: Matthew Kahn. Clerk: R. Woods. Not Reported. (525/JPT)

Jeanine Farrell, CSR# 7774, 415-515-3548 depos@westcoastreporters.net Court reporter,

Hearing is held. ARGUED. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT SAN FRANCISCO UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT MOTION FOR JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS

Defendant's motion for stay is granted.

Per CCP 128(a)(3) and (a)(8), the court has inherent authority to provide for the orderly conduct of proceedings and to control its process. "The power to stay proceedings is incidental to the power inherent in every court to control the disposition of the causes on its docket with economy of time and effort for itself, for counsel, and for litigants." (OTO, LLC v. Kho (2019) 8 Cal.5th 111, 141.)

In 2019, the Legislature passed AB 218, which amended CCP 340.1. AB 218 opened a three-year window for plaintiff to file a previously time-barred claim and it also removed the government claims presentation requirement for claims that accrued prior to January 1, 2009. Plaintiff filed this action pursuant AB 218. Defendant contends that the statute violates Article XVI, section 6 of the California Constitution because the enactment constitutes an unlawful gift of public funds which serves no public purpose. Plaintiff contends that AB 218 is constitutional.

This legal issue is presently pending before the California Court of Appeal. (See Defendant's Request for Judicial Notice, Exs. I and K [Doe v. Acalanes Union High School District, Appellate Case No. A169013; West Contra Costa United School District v. The Superior Court of Contra Costa County, Appellate Case No. A169314].)

In the interests of judicial economy, the court concludes that there is good cause to stay this action until the Court of Appeal issues its decision on the constitutionality of AB 218. A stay is appropriate pending "final appellate resolution of related actions that might conclusively determine all or some of the issues in the stayed action." (Koch-Ash v. Superior Court (Department of Transportation) (1986) 180 Cal.App.3d 689, 696.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Rochelle East; Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter:Jeanine Farrell, CSR# 7774, 415-515-3548 depos@westcoastreporters.net, Reported. (302/RBU)
